title: Testing, Static Quality, Performance, and Release · Mastery Check
summary: Harden the service-data client with behavioral tests, deterministic fixtures, performance evidence, and a clean release. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a tested and release-ready JavaScript application. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Design tests around observable behavior, changed inputs, boundaries, and failures rather than implementation lines.
Use fakes, stubs, spies, and controlled clocks only at owned boundaries without mocking away the behavior under test.
Await asynchronous outcomes, control timers and network fixtures, and fail on unhandled work deterministically.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.
// Add one runnable behavior, assertion, changed case, and evidence return per activity.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for produce a clean-environment build, supported-runtime record, accessibility checks, rollback plan, and stakeholder acceptance evidence.

content.text: A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Release Evidence symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: MDN JavaScript Guide — Controls language sequence, collections, objects, promises, modules, resource management, and advanced concepts.

hints: Name the Release Evidence precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A successful local run is sufficient release proof.

stimulus.title: Release Evidence failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A successful local run is sufficient release proof.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Produce a clean-environment build, supported-runtime record, accessibility checks, rollback plan, and stakeholder acceptance evidence.

options.text: Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
Run the boundary case, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the result because visible output is the highest authority.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Test Behavior evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Test Behavior assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: A release candidate passes the familiar 6-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Test Behavior has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Test Behavior precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

options.text: Declare inputs, observable output, ownership, and the runtime behavior being tested
Trace values, bindings, receivers, mutations, and queued work before editing code
Implement the smallest behavior and retain an assertion over the actual result
Run boundary and changed-event cases, inspect failures, and remove accidental coupling

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Release Evidence: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A release candidate passes the familiar 2-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Produce a clean-environment build, supported-runtime record, accessibility checks, rollback plan, and stakeholder acceptance evidence.

hints: Name the Release Evidence precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.
